title: Mince pies
subtitle: Sweet shortcrust pastry cases with spiced mincemeat
excerpt: Individual sweet shortcrust pastry cases filled with mincemeat and baked until golden. The pastry is enriched with icing sugar for a tender, biscuit-like crumb. Star-cut lids are traditional for the Christmas period.
sourceNotes: Mince pies have been made in Britain since the medieval period, originally containing minced meat alongside dried fruit. By the Victorian era the meat had largely disappeared, leaving the spiced dried fruit filling we recognise today. Mrs Beeton, Book of Household Management (1861), Project Gutenberg #10136, documents mince pies using a sweet suet pastry and a fruit mincemeat. The star-shaped pastry lid is a Victorian tradition. The filling is documented as mincemeat from Eliza Acton, Modern Cookery (1845), and earlier sources.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze unbaked, assembled in the tin. Bake from frozen at 200°C for 20 minutes.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: The pastry dough keeps refrigerated for 2 days. Baked mince pies keep in a tin for 4 days; warm in the oven for 5 minutes before serving.

glossaryTerms.definition: For a mince pie with a full pastry lid (rather than a star), the lid must be pressed firmly onto the moistened pastry edge to prevent it from lifting and releasing filling during baking. Dampen the rim of the pastry case with a little water or beaten egg before pressing the lid down. A star-cut lid sits on top without sealing and allows steam to escape.

content.text: Mince pies are straightforward once the pastry is made. The sweet shortcrust is more biscuit than pie pastry, which is what makes it suited to individual cases. Use a jar of good-quality mincemeat; homemade mincemeat is excellent but is a separate project. For traditional star-topped mince pies, the lid sits on top without
; for a full lid, the seal matters.

Rub the cold butter into the flour and icing sugar until the mixture resembles fine breadcrumbs. Mix in the egg yolk and lemon zest. Add 1 to 2 tablespoons of cold water and mix until the dough just comes together. Flatten into a disc, wrap, and refrigerate for 20 minutes. Preheat the oven to 200°C conventional.

Roll the pastry to 3 mm thick on a lightly floured surface. Using a round cutter slightly larger than the holes in a 12-hole bun tin, cut 12 base rounds and press gently into the tin. Fill each case with a heaped teaspoon of mincemeat, leaving room at the top for the lid. Cut star shapes or smaller round lids from the remaining pastry. Place on top of the filled cases; if using a full round lid, brush the pastry rim with beaten egg first and press to seal. Brush the tops with beaten egg. Bake for 12 to 15 minutes until pale golden. Cool in the tin for 5 minutes before easing out onto a rack. Dust with icing sugar before serving.
